place: Oldwick CDP, New Jersey, New Jersey

facts.text: Oldwick was originally called New Germantown in 1754, named by Palatine German settlers and marked by Zion Lutheran Church founded under Bishop Henry Muhlenberg.
Oldwick’s historic district, the first in Tewksbury to hit the National Register, dates back to the early 1700s and showcases English settler roots.
The Oldwick United Methodist Church, built in 1865, exemplifies Romanesque Revival architecture while the nearby Zion Evangelical Lutheran Church blends Gothic and Greek Revival styles.
The Tewksbury Historical Society, founded in 1989, preserves Oldwick’s heritage through artifacts, oral histories, and events like the annual Barn Tour.
In 1869, Farley opened the General Store, now the Chelsea Kitchen, and Jacob Apgar built the Carriage Factory, now Kitchen Caboodle, reflecting Oldwick’s 19th‑century commerce.
